Setting Up Your Site

A complete walkthrough for structuring an Outpost site from scratch — from an empty directory to a fully functioning site with pages, a blog, navigation, and custom content fields.

Before you start

Make sure Outpost is installed in the outpost/ subdirectory and you can access the admin panel. You will be creating HTML files at your site root — the same directory that contains the outpost/ folder. You do not need to restart PHP or rebuild anything — Outpost picks up new template files automatically on the next request.

Step 1: Create the directory structure

Your site root needs a partials/ directory for shared template fragments. You can also create directories for CSS, JavaScript, and images:

mkdir -p partials
mkdir css
mkdir js
mkdir images

At this point your site directory looks like:

my-site/
  partials/
  css/
  js/
  images/
  outpost/          ← already installed

Step 2: Create partials/head.html

The head.html partial is the standard place for the HTML document opening — DOCTYPE, <html>, <head>, meta tags, CSS links, and the opening <body> tag. It is included at the top of every page template.

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <outpost-meta title="My Site"></outpost-meta>
  <outpost-seo></outpost-seo>
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="/css/style.css">
</head>
<body>

Note the use of <outpost-meta title="My Site" /> — this reads per-page SEO values set in the admin. The content attribute is the default fallback used when no value has been entered.

Step 3: Create partials/nav.html

The navigation partial uses <outpost-menu> to render a menu managed in the admin. Forge (the AI site builder) auto-detects <nav> elements to register menus.

<header>
  <a href="/">
    <span data-outpost="site_name" data-scope="global">My Site</span>
  </a>
  <nav>
    <outpost-menu name="main">
      <a data-bind="href:url" data-outpost="label">Link</a>
    </outpost-menu>
  </nav>
</header>

Step 4: Create partials/footer.html

<footer>
  <p data-outpost="footer_text" data-scope="global">&copy; 2026 My Site</p>
</footer>
<script src="/js/main.js"></script>
</body>
</html>

Step 5: Create index.html (homepage)

The homepage template at your site root. This file maps to the / URL:

<outpost-include partial="head"></outpost-include>
<outpost-include partial="nav"></outpost-include>

<main>
  <!-- outpost:hero -->
  <section class="hero">
    <h1 data-outpost="hero_title">Welcome to My Site</h1>
    <p data-outpost="hero_subtitle">A brief description of what this site is about.</p>
  </section>
  <!-- /outpost:hero -->

  <!-- outpost:content -->
  <section class="content">
    <div data-outpost="page_body" data-type="raw">
      <p>Main page content goes here.</p>
    </div>
  </section>
  <!-- /outpost:content -->
</main>

<outpost-include partial="footer"></outpost-include>

The <!-- outpost:hero --> and <!-- outpost:content --> comment pairs group fields into labeled sections in the admin editor.

Step 6: Create a blog listing page

Create blog.html at the site root. This page loops over a "post" collection:

<outpost-include partial="head"></outpost-include>
<outpost-include partial="nav"></outpost-include>

<main>
  <h1 data-outpost="page_title">Blog</h1>

  <outpost-each collection="post" limit="10">
    <article>
      <h2><a data-bind="href:url" data-outpost="title">Post Title</a></h2>
      <p data-outpost="excerpt">Post excerpt...</p>
    </article>
  </outpost-each>

  <outpost-pagination></outpost-pagination>
</main>

<outpost-include partial="footer"></outpost-include>

Step 7: Create a single post template

Create post.html at the site root. The router matches URLs like /post/my-article to this template:

<outpost-include partial="head"></outpost-include>
<outpost-include partial="nav"></outpost-include>

<main>
  <outpost-single collection="post" var="post">
    <article>
      <h1 data-outpost="title">Post Title</h1>
      <img data-outpost="featured_image" data-type="image" src="placeholder.jpg" alt="">
      <div data-outpost="body" data-type="raw">Post content...</div>
    </article>
  </outpost-single>
</main>

<outpost-include partial="footer"></outpost-include>

That's it

Visit your site in a browser. The CMS discovers your templates, auto-registers all fields, and the pages appear in Admin → Pages ready to edit. No configuration files, no theme activation, no restart needed.

Tip — Use the Code Editor (Admin → Code Editor) to create and edit template files directly from the admin panel without FTP or SSH access.
